does: This paper proposes GRAM-R², a self-training approach for developing generative foundation reward models that produce both preference predictions and accompanying rationales. The method uses unlabeled data to train reward models to explicitly reason about their judgments through a self-training procedure, enabling the model to serve as a generalist reward model applicable across diverse tasks. The key contribution is combining generative modeling—where the reward model outputs natural language explanations alongside preference scores—with self-training to elicit reasoning without requiring extensive labeled preference datasets. The paper demonstrates GRAM-R² on multiple downstream applications including response ranking, task adaptation, and reinforcement learning from human feedback, showing that the approach achieves strong performance with minimal or no task-specific fine-tuning.
connects: This work builds on the recent paradigm shift toward generalist reward models that move beyond task-specific designs, extending the line of research on scaling reward modeling to broader domains. It responds to the fundamental challenge of data scarcity in reward modeling by adapting self-training approaches from semi-supervised learning to the reward modeling setting. The paper connects to prior work on preference learning and reward model pre-training, which has shown that unlabeled data can improve model performance. Additionally, it engages with growing interest in interpretable and explainable reward models, combining this with the generative modeling paradigm where models produce structured outputs including reasoning. The approach integrates ideas from instruction-tuned language models with reward modeling objectives, leveraging the reasoning capabilities of modern foundation models.
enables: This work enables several future directions in reward modeling and reinforcement learning. By creating reward models that produce explicit rationales, it sets up more interpretable and auditable systems for preference prediction and value alignment. The foundation model approach allows rapid adaptation to new tasks and domains with minimal fine-tuning, potentially democratizing reward modeling for diverse applications. The self-training framework suggests pathways for scaling reward models using unlabeled data at larger scales, addressing one of the bottlenecks in RLHF and preference-based learning systems. This foundation could support research into more efficient preference learning, multi-task reward modeling, and better understanding of reward model generalization. Additionally, models that produce reasoning alongside judgments enable downstream applications like debugging misaligned behaviors, improving human-AI collaboration in learning objectives, and developing more robust value alignment techniques.
